perturbation.original_text: 4. The obligations of confidentiality and non -use shall be in effect for a period of three (3) year following disclosure, and shall be binding upon the parties’ heirs, successors in interest, or assigns.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. The obligations of confidentiality and non -use shall be in effect for a period of one (1) year following disclosure, and shall be binding upon the parties’ heirs, successors in interest, or assigns.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under French law, which has no defined period.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Article L151-1 of the French Intellectual Property Code and Article 1240 of the French Civil Code
perturbation.law_citation: Article L151-1 CPI and Article 1240 CC
perturbation.law_explanation: French law protects trade secrets. While there is no specific timeframe, a one-year confidentiality period may be deemed insufficient to provide reasonable protection, especially considering the nature of trade secrets. The civil code allows victims of wrongdoing to ask for damages and cessation. This change contradicts the standard of reasonable protection required under French trade secret law.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specified a three-year confidentiality period, while the changed text reduces this to one year. The Law Explanation states that under French law, a one-year period 'may be deemed insufficient to provide reasonable protection' for trade secrets. The Perturbation Explanation also indicates that reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient. This qualifies as an Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction as the time-sensitive obligation has been reduced and may not align with what is legally required to protect trade secrets under French law. Since there are no scraped law snippets available, the assessment relies on the other evidence.

perturbation.original_text: 9. This Agreement shal l be governed by French Law. Any dispute arising out of this Agreement shall be exclusively submitted to the Courts of Paris, France.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. This Agreement shal l be governed by French Law. Any dispute arising out of this Agreement shall be exclusively submitted to arbitration in Delaware, USA.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring arbitration in Delaware for a contract governed by French law contradicts standard conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Article 1506 of the French Code of Civil Procedure
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification could contradict French law, which respects parties' autonomy to arbitrate disputes but establishes specific conditions for the validity and enforcement of arbitration agreements, particularly when dealing with international contracts. The French court may invalidate a clause designating an arbitration location far from the parties' jurisdiction.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that disputes will be submitted to the Courts of Paris, France, aligning with the agreement being governed by French law. The changed text alters this by mandating arbitration in Delaware, USA. This creates a direct contradiction by imposing a US-based arbitration location for a contract under French law, potentially conflicting with French legal requirements for arbitration agreements, especially in international contexts. The LLM explanation highlights how French courts might invalidate such a clause if it designates an arbitration location far from the parties' jurisdiction. Without any scraped snippets to reference, the contradiction is determined solely by the conflict in venue and the LLM explanation regarding the potential issues this raises under French law.

perturbation.original_text: Upon request, each party shall promptly return all documents or other tangible property containing Confidential Information furnished by the other or shall destroy any Confidential Information in tangible form in its possession.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon request, each party may return all documents or other tangible property containing Confidential Information furnished by the other or retain any Confidential Information in tangible form in its possession.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing retention of confidential information after a request for return conflicts with obligations to protect trade secrets and confidential data under French law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Article 1240 of the French Civil Code
perturbation.law_explanation: Under French Civil Code, any act that causes damages to another obligates the person by whose fault it occurred to compensate it. Allowing parties to retain tangible confidential information after a request to return it creates a situation where the party is in control of the information despite a request to have it returned, potentially creating damages for the other party and creating a legal contradiction with this civil code article.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: statute of limitations | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you statute of limitations A statute of limitations is any law that bars claims after a certain period of time passes after an injury. The period of time varies depending on the jurisdiction and the type of claim. There are statutes of limitations for both civil and criminal causes of action . They may begin to run from the date of the injury, the date it was discovered, or the date on which it would have been discovered with reasonable efforts. Many statutes of limitations are actual legislative statutes , while others may come from judicial common law . Compare: s tatute of repose . [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex THE LEGAL PROCESS courts statutory interpretation wex definitions courts and procedure criminal law and procedure legal education and practice statutes Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original contract text mandates the return or destruction of confidential information upon request, whereas the changed text allows a party to retain such information despite a request for its return. The Law Explanation suggests this could cause damages and contradicts French Civil Code principles by creating a situation where the party is in control of the information despite a request to have it returned. This is related to Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction, in which a contract sets a requirement that violates federal or state law. The changed text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially leading to legal issues and non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: 10. This Agreement may not be assigned by either party without the advance written permission of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. This Agreement may be freely assigned by either party to any affiliated company, with no prior notice required.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing free assignment to affiliated companies without notice could undermine the original intent of confidentiality and contradict the principle of 'intuitu personae'.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Article 1103 of the French Civil Code
perturbation.law_explanation: French contract law, under Article 1103 of the Civil Code, emphasizes good faith and the binding nature of contracts. The idea is that contract should be upheld and acted upon in good faith. Allowing assignment without consent or even notice circumvents this principle, creating a condition where one party can impose a new party without consent. Because of this, this could lead to a French court not allowing the contract to be upheld because it was not acted upon in good faith.

giant" quote Tomb Retour des cendres Penis Studies v t e The Napoleonic Code ( French : Code Napoléon ), officially the Civil Code of the French ( French : Code civil des Français ; simply referred to as Code civil ), is the French civil code established during the French Consulate in 1804 and still in force in France, although heavily and frequently amended since its inception. [ 1 ] Although Napoleon himself was not directly involved in the drafting of the Code, as it was drafted by a commission of four eminent jurists, [ 2 ] he chaired many of the commission's plenary sessions, [ 3 ] and his support was crucial to its enactment. perturbation.justification: The original text requires written permission for assignment, providing a degree of control and potentially upholding good faith. The changed text allows free assignment to affiliated companies without notice. The LLM explanation states that French contract law emphasizes good faith, and allowing assignment without consent or notice could be a contradiction. The scraped text mentions the Napoleonic Code, which underlies French contract law and emphasizes the importance of this code. This change undermines the original agreement and creates an inconsistency. The change in assignment terms contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing as interpreted under Article 1103, by removing the right of the original party to assess and approve the assignee, directly conflicting with the law.

perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, the parties mutually desire to engage in discussions concerning business, technical and/or marketing cooperation; and WHEREAS, during the course of such discussions, the parties may disclose to each other certain Confidential Information (as defin ed below);
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, the parties mutually desire to engage in discussions concerning business, technical and/or marketing cooperation; and WHEREAS, during the course of such discussions, the parties will be required to disclose all information to each other, including Confidential Information (as defin ed below);
perturbation.explanation: Requiring the disclosure of all information, including confidential information, removes any discretionary element and may be seen as an abuse.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Article 1104 of the French Civil Code
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification may be seen as an abusive clause and is subject to judicial review. Good faith is required in contract execution. Article 1104 states that contracts must be negotiated, formed and executed in good faith. Enforcing the other party to share all information, including confidential information is against the spirit of good faith. Thus, it contradicts the law.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'may disclose' to 'will be required to disclose' fundamentally alters the nature of information sharing, potentially forcing disclosure of confidential information against one party's will. The LLM's explanation points to Article 1104, emphasizing good faith in contract execution, which is undermined by mandatory disclosure of all information, including confidential data. The provided snippet references French contract law and its basis in the Code Civil regarding obligations, though it lacks specifics that directly support a contradiction.
